title: Sustained damage cycle
principles: Sustained performance is determined by the complete repeated cycle of active damage delivery, reload downtime, and ammunition availability.
Fire rate can increase immediate pressure while also consuming the magazine faster and increasing reload frequency.
Magazine size and reload time must be interpreted relative to fire rate; none determines sustained performance alone.
A large magazine is valuable when it meaningfully extends active firing time, while faster reload is most valuable when downtime occupies a significant share of the cycle.
High fire rate can create strong immediate pressure but may also increase ammunition consumption and long-duration resource demands.
Practical sustained output depends on maintaining valid target contact; only discuss recoil, accuracy, spread, or transitions when explicitly supplied.
Reload improvements reduce forced downtime, and their value grows when the weapon has a short active firing window or long interruption.
Magazine improvements delay reload interruptions, but their value diminishes when the original magazine already lasts substantially longer than supported engagement windows.
Ammo capacity, ammo pickup, and ammunition cost determine whether the firing cycle can continue across extended use.
Do not infer ammunition efficiency from fire rate alone when pickup or per-attack cost is absent.
Sustained damage describes useful pressure over repeated cycles, not uninterrupted firing for an entire mission.
A sustained firing rhythm does not automatically make sustained_damage the primary job when the complete mechanic more distinctly supports another function.
exceptions: Do not treat theoretical fire rate as practical sustained output when firing behavior limits real application.
Do not infer ammunition starvation without capacity, pickup, cost, or explicit evidence.
Do not merge chained, radial, or secondary damage into the main cycle unless explicitly supplied.
